current_fact.fact_text: In 1879, a conflict over nitrate-rich deserts erupted as Bolivia declared war on Chile, and Chile in turn declared war on Peru, igniting the War of the Pacific. What began as a dispute over taxes on a mining company reshaped coastlines and borders, leaving Bolivia landlocked and forever changing South America’s map.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

current_fact.match_reason: A South American resource war and shifting borders pair with an episode about Latin identity, resistance, and political storytelling.

queue.fact_text: In 2007, the cruise ship MS Sea Diamond struck a volcanic reef near Nea Kameni in the Santorini caldera and slowly sank, tilting toward the crater like a modern relic being reclaimed by the Aegean. Most of the 1,600 people aboard escaped, but two missing passengers turned the ship into a sunken memorial beneath one of Greece’s most iconic views.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

queue.match_reason: A shipwreck in the heart of the Mediterranean fits an episode that sails sonically across the same sea, mixing coastal stories and sounds.

queue.fact_text: Also in 1991, Atlantic Southeast Airlines Flight 2311, an Embraer EMB 120 commuter flight, crashed near Brunswick, Georgia after a propeller malfunction, killing all 23 people on board. Among them were U.S. Senator John Tower and NASA astronaut Sonny Carter, making the accident a sudden intersection of politics, space exploration, and everyday travel.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: A tragic transport accident involving an astronaut is paired with a reflective, cinematic episode about risky journeys and the thin line between adventure and loss.

queue.fact_text: In 1971, the Janatha Vimukthi Peramuna in Sri Lanka launched an armed Marxist insurrection against the United Front government, led largely by idealistic rural youth. For weeks, the island saw village police stations fall and roads blocked, as a generation tried to rewrite their country’s future with homemade weapons and clandestine cells.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

queue.match_reason: A youth-led revolt against the status quo echoes an episode dedicated to breaking out of boxes, with conscious rap as its soundtrack.

queue.fact_text: At the 37th Academy Awards in 1965, the lavish musical "My Fair Lady" won Best Picture, triumphing in a year that also included "Dr. Strangelove" and "Mary Poppins." Its transformation story—from Cockney flower seller to high-society lady—mirrored Hollywood’s love of reinvention, costume, and the magic of a well-timed song.

episode.title: Sedna FM #03 - Beautiful as You Feel
episode.description: An episode about self-acceptance and inner beauty. Music that reminds us we are beautiful as we feel, embracing our authentic selves. Warm, uplifting sounds that celebrate individuality.
songs: The The - this is the day
DCTL - Deep Tannin f(eat Rafael Leafar)

queue.match_reason: A story about external makeover and social class is matched with an episode that flips the script toward inner beauty and self-acceptance.

queue.fact_text: In 1942, Adolf Hitler issued Führer Directive No. 41, planning Case Blue, a massive German offensive toward the Caucasus oil fields and the city of Stalingrad. On paper it looked like a clean arrow slicing across Soviet maps; in reality it led to one of history’s bloodiest urban battles and the beginning of the Reich’s collapse.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

queue.match_reason: A rigid, overconfident military plan that unraveled pairs with an episode about questioning certainty and the cost of not rethinking assumptions.

queue.fact_text: In 1936, an F5 tornado tore through Tupelo, Mississippi as part of the Tupelo–Gainesville tornado outbreak, killing 233 people in the city alone. The storm’s violence was so extreme that entire neighborhoods were erased, yet a young boy named Elvis Presley survived in one of the hardest-hit areas, growing up to reshape music history.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: A raw, destructive burst of natural energy is matched with a high-voltage rock episode, translating atmospheric fury into guitar-driven electricity.

queue.fact_text: In 1945, as World War II waned, Yugoslav leader Josip Broz Tito signed an agreement allowing the "temporary" entry of Soviet troops into Yugoslav territory. Within a few years, he would defy Stalin and chart a fiercely independent socialist path, turning that temporary welcome into a cautionary tale about big neighbors and small countries.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A pivotal, reflective geopolitical decision about allies and autonomy fits an introspective episode about transitions, vulnerability, and long-term consequences.

queue.fact_text: In 1818, the Battle of Maipú secured Chilean independence as Bernardo O'Higgins and José de San Martín’s forces defeated the Spanish royalists near Santiago. The victory was so decisive that O'Higgins reportedly embraced San Martín on the battlefield, saying, "Glory to the savior of Chile," sealing both a republic and a friendship in the dust.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: A liberation battle in Latin America aligns with a freedom-focused episode on Afrobeat, another sound born from struggles against colonial power.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1614, Pocahontas, a Powhatan woman already taken to England and baptized as Rebecca, married English tobacco planter John Rolfe in Virginia. Their union, both deeply personal and heavily politicized, briefly eased tensions between colonists and Native peoples in what became known as the "Peace of Pocahontas."

episode.title: Sedna FM #04 - Queen of Spades
episode.description: Tuning in from the planet of irrational love. Exploring love's most vulnerable side — the kind that awakens the child within us. A diverse music selection ranging from electric blues to downtempo, soul, and jazz. A tribute to the feeling that has moved artists to create the most beautiful works. Love makes us vulnerable, and there is no art without vulnerability.
songs: Alabama Shakes - Gimme All Your Love
Queens of the stone age - Make it wit chu
Etta James - I'd Rather Go Blind
Eric Clapton - Have you ever loved a woman

queue.match_reason: A complex, vulnerable marriage at the crossroads of cultures fits an episode that explores love’s irrational, risky, and tender edges.

queue.fact_text: In 1998, Japan opened the Akashi Kaikyō Bridge, whose central span stretches nearly two kilometers, making it the longest suspension bridge span in the world. Engineers had to adjust its design mid-construction after the Kobe earthquake slightly shifted the anchoring shores farther apart, turning seismic chaos into a structural recalculation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Wabi Sabi
episode.description: Wabi-sabi is a story about a handmade mug, a small mistake, and the idea that imperfection can be something to keep—not correct. In this Morning Drop, Gaia shares a moment that didn’t go to plan, and four tracks that echo the same spirit: rough takes, studio accidents, lo-fi textures, and quiet surprises. A soft, thoughtful selection moving between warm electronics, classic riffs, and raw edges.A reminder that not everything needs to be polished. Sometimes, it’s better that way.

queue.match_reason: A record-breaking bridge subtly lengthened by an earthquake embodies engineering wabi-sabi—adapting to imperfection—mirroring the episode’s embrace of flaws and recalibration.

queue.fact_text: In 1951, Julius and Ethel Rosenberg were sentenced to death in the United States for passing atomic secrets to the Soviet Union, becoming symbols of Cold War paranoia and contested justice. Their trial mixed science, espionage, and fear, leaving historians to argue for decades over how much they actually revealed—and what their execution really proved.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: A story of secrecy, surveillance, and technology in the atomic age fits an episode questioning our modern surveillance-adjacent habits and techno-dependence.

queue.fact_text: In 1621, after delivering its human cargo to the New World, the Mayflower raised its worn sails and left Plymouth, Massachusetts, returning to England almost empty. The ship that had become a symbol of beginnings quietly exited the story, leaving its passengers to face a new continent without their wooden link to home.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

queue.match_reason: A famous transatlantic ship’s farewell voyage pairs naturally with an episode recorded at sea, celebrating the strange intimacy of life aboard a boat.

queue.fact_text: In 919, the Fatimid heir al-Qa'im bi-Amr Allah marched from Raqqada at the head of an army, beginning the second Fatimid invasion of Egypt. The campaign was not just a military move but a religious and political gamble to project a new Shi'a caliphate into the heart of Sunni Abbasid territory along the Nile.

episode.title: Morning Drops #02 - Waves of the Maghreb
episode.description: Oriental vibes guided by Yasmin. Discovering curious facts about the Maghreb, together with its fascinating music! A wonderful trip with a selection of songs from North Africa.
songs: Nass El Ghiwane - El Madi Fate

queue.match_reason: A North African dynasty pushing east toward Egypt connects with an episode exploring Maghreb culture and sound, both traveling across Arabic-speaking lands.

queue.fact_text: In 2018, U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement raided a slaughterhouse in Tennessee, detaining almost 100 undocumented Hispanic workers in one of the largest workplace raids in modern U.S. history. The operation left children returning from school to find empty homes, sparking local church mobilizations and new debates about the human cost of immigration policy.

episode.title: Morning Drops #13 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

queue.match_reason: A raid targeting Latino workers in the U.S. is paired with an episode that honors Latin urban culture and history, giving voice to communities often reduced to statistics.

queue.fact_text: On 5 April 2007, the alien curator’s favorite detail: as the Sea Diamond slowly listed in the Santorini caldera, local boats and coastline residents improvised a rescue that saved over 1,500 people in just a few hours. The ship would become a new artificial reef, but first the island briefly turned itself into an unplanned flotilla of lifeboats.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: The caldera setting and improvised seaborne rescue resonate with an episode about travel, perspective shifts, and the way journeys can suddenly change course.

queue.fact_text: Back in 1942’s Indian Ocean raid, an overlooked detail is how Ceylon’s civilian harbor workers and local air defenses scrambled alongside Royal Navy ships, trying to track incoming aircraft with limited radar. In a region better known for tea plantations and temples, the day briefly turned Colombo’s port into a high-tech chessboard of searchlights, sirens, and coded radio.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

queue.match_reason: A port city suddenly turned into a war ‘destination’ pairs with a sonically diverse voyage episode, hinting at how harbors connect worlds in both peace and conflict.

queue.fact_text: In the Cold War’s shadow, Tito’s Yugoslavia experimented with a non-aligned path, hosting leaders from newly independent nations who did not want to choose between Washington and Moscow. The 1945 agreement letting Soviet troops in was just the opening act for a country that would later help found the Non-Aligned Movement and turn neutrality into a kind of quiet defiance.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

queue.match_reason: Yugoslavia’s balancing act between global powers echoes an episode from non-aligned Thailand, focused on discipline, self-determination, and carving your own path.

queue.fact_text: Across these April 5th stories—from hidden rocks detonated under cold Canadian currents to gamma-ray eyes launched into orbit—Earth reveals itself as a planet constantly tinkering with its own limits. Bridges grow longer, ships sink and become reefs, revolts rise and fall, and sometimes a single decision, launch, or love story quietly reshapes the map for centuries.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: A closing, big-picture reflection on April 5 as a day of turning points aligns with Sedna FM’s very first episode—a point of origin for everything that followed on this tiny radio planet.

published.fact_text: In 2010, Space Shuttle Discovery embarked on mission STS-131, a pre-dawn launch that carried racks of science experiments, a new sleeping compartment, and thousands of kilograms of supplies to the International Space Station. Among its crew was Naoko Yamazaki, one of Japan's first female astronauts, turning the dark Florida sky into a brief artificial sunrise for orbit-bound explorers.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A literal space mission pairs with an episode about reimagining 'space' as creative possibility, echoing the ISS as a lab for new beginnings.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1991, Space Shuttle Atlantis launched on STS-37 to deploy the Compton Gamma Ray Observatory, a telescope designed to catch some of the universe’s most energetic and invisible light. For nearly a decade, Compton revealed mysterious gamma-ray bursts and high-energy phenomena, turning the whole sky into a dynamic, flickering canvas.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

published.match_reason: The cosmic, slow-building mystery of gamma rays matches an evening mix that begins in deep, atmospheric textures and expands across sonic landscapes.

published.fact_text: In 2009, North Korea launched the Kwangmyŏngsŏng-2 rocket over Japan, a single streak across the sky that triggered diplomatic shockwaves on Earth. The flight blurred the line between space exploration and geopolitical theater, showing how a lone satellite test can unsettle an entire planet’s sense of security.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A contentious satellite launch and the politics around it resonate with an episode centered on tension, protest, and global power struggles.

published.fact_text: In 1958, engineers in Canada blew up an underwater mountain: Ripple Rock, a hidden twin-peaked seamount that had ripped open the hulls of dozens of ships in the Seymour Narrows. With one of the largest non-nuclear controlled explosions in history, a lethal piece of the seafloor was reshaped in a single, thundering moment so ships could pass more safely.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: A dramatic underwater engineering feat fits an episode built around immersion, water-like movement, and the hidden dramas beneath the surface.

published.fact_text: On 5 April 1942, Japanese aircraft swooped over Colombo, Ceylon in the Indian Ocean raid, sinking British cruisers Cornwall and Dorsetshire and setting fires across the harbor. The attack briefly turned the turquoise waters around Sri Lanka into a battlefield, revealing how even idyllic islands can be pulled into global tides of war.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'è Profondo il Mare

published.match_reason: The contrast between peaceful sea and sudden flames mirrors the 'fog and fire' duality in the episode’s mood and storytelling.

published.fact_text: In 1879, a conflict over nitrate-rich deserts erupted as Bolivia declared war on Chile, and Chile in turn declared war on Peru, igniting the War of the Pacific. What began as a dispute over taxes on a mining company reshaped coastlines and borders, leaving Bolivia landlocked and forever changing South America’s map.
